info.topictitle: A2.1.7 Evidence for a last universal common ancestor

markdown:
## What Is LUCA?


<definition term="LUCA (Last Universal Common Ancestor)">The last universal common ancestor of all present-day organisms, representing the root of the tree of life.</definition>

1. LUCA likely lived around **4 billion years ago**.
2. Its existence is inferred from **universal features** shared across all life forms today.

![Tracing LUCA](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/6af05d1644fac3507a4b2895fdc65215b7d26cac-3000x1500.png)

## Evidence For LUCA: The Genetic Code


<definition term="Genetic code">The genetic code is a system that translates DNA instructions into **proteins**, the essential building blocks of life.</definition>

1. One of the strongest pieces of evidence for LUCA is the **near-universality of the genetic code** across all organisms.
2. The **genetic code** is a system that translates **DNA sequences into proteins**, essential for life.DNA is composed of four nucleotide bases: **adenine (A), thymine (T), cytosine (C), and guanine (G)**.
3. These bases are read in groups of three, called **codons**.

<definition term="Codon">A **codon** is a sequence of **three nucleotides** in DNA or RNA that specifies a particular **amino acid** or a **start/stop signal** during protein synthesis.</definition>

<callout type="example">The** codon** AUG codes for methionine and also serves as a **start signal** for protein assembly.</callout>

### Why Is the Genetic Code Universal?


1. The genetic code is **remarkably consistent** across almost all organisms.
2. Even vastly different organisms (humans and _E. coli_) use the **same ribosomes** to translate genetic instructions.

<callout type="example">* The codon **UUU** codes for the amino acid **phenylalanine** in humans, yeast, and even bacteria. 
* This shared "language" strongly suggests that all life on Earth **shares **a **common origin**.</callout>

## What This Tells Us About LUCA


1. If life arose independently multiple times, we would expect **different genetic codes**, like different human languages.
2. Instead, the **shared genetic code** points to a **single ancestral organism** (LUCA) that passed it down to all descendants.

<embed type="inline" url="https://www.youtube.com/embed/p9XHI_26cPE?si=Da-fNpt7GpVD0nK1">LUCA</embed>

<callout type="self_review">* Why is the genetic code considered universal, and what does this imply about the origins of life?
* What evidence supports the existence of LUCA, and what do we know about its environment?
* How might natural selection and environmental factors have led to the extinction of other life forms on early Earth?</callout>
